svg.title: Flyte execution flow for beginners
svg.desc: Step by step: a Kafka message triggers a Flyte workflow, Flyte builds a job spec from a Docker image, resource config and secrets, Kubernetes runs the job as a pod, and on completion the next stage is notified.

g.onclick: sendPrompt('What is Kafka and why is it used here?')
g.text: Step 1 — A message arrives in Kafka
Kafka is a message queue. It holds a job (e.g. "parse this page").
Our consumer reads it and picks up the page_id + trace_id.

g.onclick: sendPrompt('What is Flyte and what is FlyteRemote?')
g.text: Step 2 — Consumer tells Flyte to run a workflow
Flyte is a workflow engine. The consumer calls
FlyteRemote.execute() — like pressing "Run" on the job.

svg.text: Step 3 — Flyte prepares the job using three things

g.onclick: sendPrompt('What is a Docker image in simple terms?')
sendPrompt('What is a pod template in Kubernetes?')

g.text: A Pod is a single running instance of your job on the cluster.
It starts the Docker image, injects secrets as env vars,
runs the task function, then writes output to blob storage.

g.onclick: sendPrompt('What happens after a Flyte pod finishes?')
g.text: Step 5 — Job done, next stage triggered
Flyte sees the pod finished successfully.
It publishes a new Kafka message to kick off the next stage.
